name: CodelyTV/java-ddd-example
rationale: Clean DDD skeleton with CQRS, hexagonal ports/adapters, well-organized packages by bounded context. Actively maintained, pure Java with small FreeMarker templates.
notes: Actively maintained Spring Boot + Gradle project; excellent structural showcase for DDD patterns.

name: thombergs/buckpal
rationale: Companion code for 'Get Your Hands Dirty on Clean Architecture' book by Tom Hombergs. Minimal, intentionally small project demonstrating clean hexagonal layering. Pure Java 100%.
notes: Well-known in Java community; used as a reference implementation for clean hexagonal architecture.

name: anton-liauchuk/educational-platform
rationale: Java-only modular monolith with DDD bounded contexts and CQRS. Good separation of modules, clean package structure. Active public repo.
notes: Featured in awesome-software-architecture list as a Java DDD modular monolith example.

name: quarkusio/quarkus-quickstarts
rationale: Official Quarkus quickstart collection covering REST, JPA, Kafka, reactive patterns. Well-structured, maintained by Red Hat/Quarkus team. 62% Java, 36% HTML (mostly Panache templates).
notes: Very actively maintained. Each quickstart is a self-contained module; useful for framework architecture diversity.

name: piomin/sample-spring-microservices-new
rationale: Demo of Spring Cloud microservices (Eureka, Config, Gateway, Feign). Pure Java. Some cross-cutting concerns intermingled but representative of typical Spring Cloud architecture.
notes: Author Piotr Minkowski is a well-known Spring expert blogger; repo actively updated.

name: google/guava
rationale: Google's core Java library; exemplary library architecture with extremely clean internal design, well-separated packages (collections, cache, concurrency, I/O). Industry gold standard.
notes: One of the most-starred Java repos. Pure Java, very large—good for large-size GT entries.

name: FasterXML/jackson-databind
rationale: Core JSON data-binding library for Java. Complex but well-evolved codebase with some known design complexity in serialization chain. Mixed quality signals—deep inheritance, many extension points.
notes: Most popular JSON library for Java. Some components are well-structured; core serializer chain is complex/god-class territory.

name: apache/camel
rationale: Massive Apache integration framework implementing hundreds of EIP components. Some components well-designed; overall routing engine has high complexity. Good for large-scale diversity.
notes: 6000+ stars, actively maintained (80k+ commits). Java 99%. Represents complex real-world framework architecture.

name: apache/commons-lang
rationale: Well-known Apache utility library with clean, stable API design. Good test coverage, straightforward class hierarchy. Java 98%, actively maintained.
notes: Among the most-used Java libraries. Represents good library architecture with small-to-medium size.

name: apache/commons-collections
rationale: Apache Commons Collections extends the Java Collections Framework. Well-organized by package (bag, bidimap, collection, map, queue). Clean, mature library code.
notes: 722 stars, Java 99.5%. Small-medium sized library with good structural quality.

name: apache/tomcat
rationale: Apache Tomcat is a large, mature Java servlet container. Historically monolithic with complex internal design (Catalina, Coyote, Jasper layers). Good mix of well-designed interfaces and legacy complexity.
notes: 8.1k stars, Java 97.7%. Active development. Represents large-scale legacy/mature monolithic server architecture.

name: apache/wicket
rationale: Apache Wicket is a mature stateful component-based Java web framework. Complex internal state-management architecture; Java-heavy with some HTML/JS. Known for deep component tree.
notes: 785 stars, active. Represents alternative (non-Spring) enterprise Java web framework architecture.

name: spring-projects/spring-batch
rationale: Spring Batch is a well-structured batch processing framework with clean job/step/reader/writer/processor abstraction. Pure Java, actively maintained by Spring team.
notes: 2.9k stars, Java 100%. Part of the official Spring portfolio, well-documented architecture.

name: spring-projects/spring-framework
rationale: The foundational Spring Framework. Extremely large and complex multi-module project. Well-designed core IoC/AOP/MVC modules but massive scale with intricate internal dependencies.
notes: 58.7k stars. Java 97.7%, 2.1% Kotlin. Represents the canonical large enterprise Java framework.

name: igniterealtime/Openfire
rationale: Real-time XMPP server with a large legacy codebase. Known for deep class hierarchies, high coupling in core server components, and god-class patterns in MultiUserChatServiceImpl (3600+ lines). Represents large legacy monolith.
notes: 3k stars. Java 95.1%. Active releases. MultiUserChatServiceImpl.java alone is 3618 lines—strong NEG signal.

name: openmrs/openmrs-core
rationale: OpenMRS is a large healthcare electronic medical records system built as a layered Java monolith. Known for high coupling across API/dao/service layers, complex service layer design, and legacy anti-patterns.
notes: 1.8k stars. Java 98.5%. Widely cited as an example of organic growth leading to architectural debt.

name: javaparser/javaparser
rationale: Java source code parser producing an AST. Well-designed visitor pattern use; some complexity in generated AST node classes. Widely used tool, well-maintained.
notes: 6.1k stars. Java 99.4%. 10k commits. Mixed quality: visitor design is clean; generated AST node files are large.

name: remkop/picocli
rationale: Picocli is a modern Java CLI framework notable for its single-file design, annotation-based API, and excellent documentation. Well-structured, actively maintained. Good example of a focused, small-scope library.
notes: 5.4k stars. Java 95.8%, Groovy 1.6%. Latest release April 2025.

name: iluwatar/java-design-patterns
rationale: Curated collection of design patterns implemented in Java. Each pattern is a standalone module with clean implementation. Highest-starred Java repository in scope. Great architectural diversity.
notes: 93.9k stars. Java 99.4%. Multi-module Maven project—each pattern is isolated and well-documented.

name: BroadleafCommerce/BroadleafCommerce
rationale: Java Spring e-commerce framework with a very large, monolithic codebase. Known for complex extension model, deep class hierarchies with Broadleaf's own ORM patterns, and high coupling. 85% Java but includes JS/CSS.
notes: 1.9k stars. Actively maintained. Java 84.8%. Represents complex enterprise e-commerce platform with architectural debt.

name: shashirajraja/shopping-cart
rationale: Traditional Java Servlet/JSP e-commerce shopping cart with JDBC. No clear layering, controller logic mixed with business logic, raw JDBC—classic example of un-architected CRUD web app.
notes: 320 stars. Java 96.7%. Used in educational content. Good NEG example of non-layered servlet-based architecture.

name: yangzongzhuan/RuoYi-Vue
rationale: Popular Chinese admin panel generator (RuoYi) built on Spring Boot + Vue. Backend Java is heavily procedural, no DDD, mixed concerns in service layer. Represents typical CRUD admin panel architecture.
notes: 2.5k stars. Java backend 54.3%. High coupling between security/controller/service. Known in Chinese open-source community.

name: sourcegraph/training-java-monolith-refactor
rationale: Sourcegraph training application explicitly designed to demonstrate legacy JSP/DAO anti-patterns including raw JDBC, DAO pattern without separation, and traditional JSP-based architecture. Created for refactoring exercises.
notes: Java 97.2%. Published 2025. Intentional legacy anti-patterns: raw JDBC, God-service pattern, no layering discipline.

name: Netflix/Hystrix
rationale: Netflix's circuit-breaker and latency-tolerance library. In maintenance mode (not archived). Well-designed command pattern but some complexity in observable/reactive chains. 24.5k stars—widely referenced.
notes: 24.5k stars. Java 98.5%, Clojure 1.4%. In maintenance mode per readme but not GitHub-archived. Historical reference for resilience patterns.

name: colinbut/monolith-enterprise-application
rationale: Skeleton of a traditional J2EE enterprise application using Spring MVC, Hibernate, ActiveMQ, Quartz. Intentionally models 'stereotypical backend enterprise application' characteristics. 100% Java.
notes: Pure Java 100%. Demonstrates classic layered J2EE monolith patterns without modern clean architecture.

name: kbastani/spring-cloud-microservice-example
rationale: End-to-end cloud-native microservices example using Spring Cloud, demonstrating service discovery, config server, API gateway, event-driven communication. Demonstrates real microservices patterns.
notes: Well-known Spring Cloud demo. Represents microservices pattern diversity for the dataset.

name: SvenWoltmann/hexagonal-architecture-java
rationale: Pure Java hexagonal architecture demo (no framework) using RESTEasy, Hibernate, Undertow. Clean separation of domain/application/infrastructure layers without Spring. Good contrast to Spring-based examples.
notes: Java 100%. Part of HappyCoders tutorial series. Represents framework-free hexagonal approach.

name: mattia-battiston/clean-architecture-example
rationale: Java clean architecture example with layered module separation: entities, use cases, adapters, frameworks. Demonstrates testing pyramid with isolated layers. Accompanied by presentation slides.
notes: 1.8k stars. Well-documented clean architecture reference for Java.

name: thingsboard/thingsboard
rationale: ThingsBoard is an open-source IoT platform with 16.5k stars. Java backend (59.7%) with rule engine, device management, and data processing. Large, complex system that started monolithic and evolved toward microservices.
notes: 16.5k stars. Java 59.7%, TypeScript 22%, HTML 9.5%. Not pure Java but Java is the dominant backend language.

name: la-team/light-admin
rationale: Pluggable CRUD admin library for JPA applications. DSL-based configuration, Spring Data integration. Stagnant development since ~2014, minimal architectural discipline—PoC-level quality.
notes: 661 stars. Represents stagnant CRUD admin panel architecture with questionable long-term design.
